EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado hit the city of Moreland along Pioneer Road between 800 and 900 West between 6:50 and 7:00 pm MDT On June 23rd.   four homes received damage.  A shed at one home was destroyed and a garage to the back of another home was destroyed.  A third home had wood sidign removed and damaged trees.  A camper was flipped over and was on it's top at a 4th home.   The Jensen Grove Park near Blackfoot had 19 trees either snapped or uprooted by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado hit the city of Moreland along Pioneer Road between 800 and 900 West between 6:50 and 7:00 pm MDT On June 23rd.   four homes received damage.  A shed at one home was destroyed and a garage to the back of another home was destroyed.  A third home had wood sidign removed and damaged trees.  A camper was flipped over and was on it's top at a 4th home.   A storm survey was done and it was determined to be an EF0 tornado.  The Jensen Grove Park near Blackfoot had 19 trees either snapped or uprooted by the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Teton River near St Anthony was above flood stage several times in June beginning on June 7th and continuing off on on through the 30th.   The highest crest of 6.61 feet occurred on June 15th.   Numerous agricultural fields and low level lands were flooded affecting some farm equipment.  Minor bank erosion occurred along the levee.  The Henry's Fork at St Anthonywas at flood stage from June 7th through June 9th.  The river crested at a record level of 8.84 feet on June 8th.  One home 2 miles east of St Anthony was flooded with 3 to 4 feet of water in the basement.   Some outbuildings were flooded.  Keefer Park in St Anthony flooded and a city water pipe was damaged from debris in the river.  Extensive flooding of lowlands and pasturelands occurred near St Anthony.  A portion of South River Road was covered with floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Snake River at Blackfoot was above flood stage from June 1st through June 19th.  It was below flood stage from June 13th through June 16th.  Water levels reached moderate flood stage and crested at 11.79 feet on the evening of June 10th.   There was widespread floodign in the Riverton area resulting in the closure of Riverton Road with several homes threatened.  A number of homes and access roads were threatened in the Thomas area near Riverbend Road and Wilson Road.  Berm construction and sandbagging appeared to have been successful in those areas.  Archery Range Road near the Rose Overpass was submerged and impassable for a period.  In Blackfoot, sub-water seepage into Jensens Grove caused minor flooding of the park.  Flooding of lowland and agricultural fields occurred adjacent to the river between Rose and Tilden Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above average precipitation during the spring season combined with an accelerated rate of snow melt during late May to early June led to saturated soils across northern Idaho.  The unstable hillside conditions eventually led to a large rockslide near Bonners Ferry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rockslide occurred across a busy railroad line approximately 15 miles east of Bonners Ferry.  The slide was about 30 feet high and reportedly occurred during the afternoon on Sunday June 12th.  Crews worked through the night to clear the slide and the section of railroad was opened at 2210PST on Monday June 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Due to capacity issues at Lucky Peak Reservoir in Boise, officials were forced to increase flow on the Boise River causing the channel to go above flood stage during the day on the 3rd of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Boise River experienced minor flooding due to a planned release from Lucky Peak Dam. the river crested at 10.03 feet around 300 PM MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Breneau River went out of it's banks from the 9th to the 10th due to heavy rain and snow melt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River gauge observations and reports from local officials indicated the Bruneau River experienced minor flooding from Hot Springs to just west of Bruneau near State Highway 51. The river crested at 9.39 feet at 130 AM MDT on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowpack melting combing with rains allowed many rivers to go above flood stage and flooding to occur in much of southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Portneuf River at Pocatello reached flood stage of 8.5 feet on May 8th and remained over flood stage through the rest of the month peaking at 9.95 feet on May 28th.  Extensive low land flooding occurred in Inkom.  Pasture and livestock corrals were flooded in the vicinity of the Portneuf River and marsh Creek confluence.  Lowland flooding occurred along the river between Portneuf Gap and the Cheyenne Avenue Bridge in Pocatello.  Flooding of lowlands and a recreation trail in Pocatello occurred from Sacajawea Park to Pacific Recylcing Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains and snowmelt caused many rivers to reach flood stage and many counties experienced flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Henry's Fork River near Rexburg reached flood stage of 9.5 feet on May 15th and remained above through the end of the month. The river peaked at 10.96 feet on May 26th. Extensive low land flooding occurred. Beaver Dick Park was flooded as were some river access recreational areas. Water covered portions of the road in the Warm Slough Access to a depth of around one foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extensive snowfall melt combined with rain in May caused minor flooding in several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive lowland and field flooding occurred along the Bear River upstream of Stewart Dam.  Two homes in the dingle area surrounded by water.  Water covered a portion of Pegram Road near the town of Pegram.  Water flowed across Poverty Flat Road near the intersection with Pegram Road.  Extensive lowland flooding also occurred along the Thomas Fork near the Wyoming Border.  Water flowed across county roads and some livestock corrals were inundated with water.  Humerous small creeks draining east into Bear River and Bear Lake exceeded their banks and caused damage and washouts  along back country roads.  Montpelier Creek caused flooding of some prpoerties in the canyon east of the town of Montpelier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extensive snowfall melt combined with rain in May caused minor flooding in several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive lowland flooding along Pine Creek and damage to some secondary roads.  Numerous mud slides along Highway 31 leading to Pine Creek Pass.  Rainey Creek exceeded its banks and caused minor flooding of lowland and some driveways in the Swan Valley area.  Fall Creek was periodically out of its banks and impacted a dirt road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extensive snowfall melt combined with rain in May caused minor flooding in several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small stream flooding occurred in the Slug Creek and Diamond Creek drainages flowing toward Wyoming.  Tincup Creek overflowed its banks and flooded some fields.  Many back country roads were damaged or washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extensive snowfall melt combined with rain in May caused minor flooding in several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of pasture and agricultural fields occurred.  Some roads in the Kilgore area were flooded and several roads werre raised to avoid the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extensive snowfall melt combined with rain in May caused minor flooding in several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive road damage and washouts occurred along Kelly Canyon Road.  Extensive lawland and agricultural field flooding occurred and a few properties had minor flooding in yards.  One home 3 miles east of Roberts just off Highway 48 had water in the basement.  Teh Menan Buttes Public Access boat ramp flooded and a recreational day use area and boat ramp adjacent to Highway 48 flooded.  Levees were raised in the Roberts area and Scotts Slough by the Menan Buttes Bridge to avoid flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a very wet April and May, warmer June temperatures began to melt an above normal mountain snowpack increasing flows into already swollen river basins. |This caused minor to locally moderate flooding along Lake Pend Oreille and Pend Oreille River along with several of its smaller tributaries. Agriculture fields, recreation sites, roadways, as well as homes were impacted by the flooding.  Flooding along the Pend Oreille River and on the Pend Oreille Lake through the first week of July began on June 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor spring flooding occurred along the Pend Oreille River, Clark Fork River, and Lake Pend Oreille. Johnson Creek Road on the south side of the Clark Fork River had some sloughing. In central and western areas of the county, Priest River inundated portions of LeClerc Road just east of Oldtown as well as Dufort Road, just east of Priest Lake. Numerous docks and lakeside yards were flooded by high water levels along the shores of Lake Pend Oreille. In Sandpoint, ID the basement of the Old Power House was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonally warm air ahead of a strong cold front provided the ingredients for severe thunderstorm development over parts of South Central Idaho on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twin Falls ASOS at Kimberly reported a wind gust of 68 MPH and the Idaho Department of Transportation automated observation system on the Hansen Bridge reported a gust of 67 MPH as a line of thunderstorms passed through the Twin Falls area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of surface heating and weak ascent ahead of a compact upper-level low drifting into north-central Oregon resulted in scattered thunderstorms over Northeastern Oregon on the afternoon of the 28th.  Meanwhile, very dry boundary layer conditions were developing over North Idaho.  By the late afternoon and early evening, southerly steering flow directed the storms into the Idaho Panhandle.  While gusty outflow winds were common with most storms, two storms were thought to have produced a microburst resulting in localized damage in each of Nez Perce and Kootenai Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst was thought to be responsible for snapping twenty consecutive power poles between the Setters substation and Highway 95 along Setters Road.   It is inconclusive whether the initial wind gust snapped only a few poles or was the direct cause for damaging all the poles.  It is speculated that a few poles broke in response to the microburst resulting in a domino effect to the remaining poles due to the absence of additional damage in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A early October snowstorm caused extensive power outages as snapping tree branches fell on power lines in Idaho Falls.  3 to 6 inches of snow fell in the Idaho Falls area with 4 to 12 inches in areas of the Southeast Highlands.  Tree branches also reported snapping in the Ashton area as well as Soda Springs.  7 inches fell at Soda Springs with 6 inches in Ashton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple power outages were caused by heavy snow in the Idaho Falls area.  6 inches fell in Idaho Falls with downed tree branches and limbs collapsing on power lines.  1500 people reaported without power as of 4 pm in the region.  1 to 3 inches were reported in Rexburg...Rigby...and Shelley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slug Creek Divide SNOTEL reported 11 inches of snow.  Emigrant Summit reported 5 inches and Giveout 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure off the Pacific Northwest Coast steered a deep fetch of subtropical moisture into the Idaho Panhandle which persisted from March 29th through early April. This resulted in several rounds of moderate to heavy precipitation, which primarily fell as rain below 6000 feet.  Mild temperatures and breezy winds accompanying the subtropical moisture feed resulted in excessive mid-elevation snowmelt.  The combination of heavy rainfall and excessive snow melt resulted in sharp rises in most basins within the Central Idaho Panhandle and Idaho Palouse.   In addition, saturated soils became unstable in some locations resulting in debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive runoff into Hauser Lake caused the lake to expand out of its banks flooding portions of Hauser Lake Road between Matheson and Ragged Ridge roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage and warning level winds occurred with the passage of a cold front the evening of March 10th.  Two trees were downed into a house in Burley.  Several trees downed at Burley parks and golf course.  Power lines were downed south of Rupert along Idaho route 24.  Much of Minidoka County had power outages causing a black out for nearly 2 hours.   The city of Rupert had a complete power outage.  Power was restored to many areas by 8 pm.  A roof was blown off a building in American Falls into power lines with power outages in the city.   The winds caused a power outage in Bear Lake County on the west side of the valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Police dispatchers reported a deck blown over a house in the city of Chubbuck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage and warning level winds occurred with the passage of a cold front the evening of March 10th.  Two trees were downed into a house in Burley.  Several trees downed at Burley parks and golf course.  Power lines were downed south of Rupert along Idaho route 24.  Much of Minidoka County had power outages causing a black out for nearly 2 hours.   The city of Rupert had a complete power outage.  Power was restored to many areas by 8 pm.  A roof was blown off a building in American Falls into power lines with power outages in the city.   The winds caused a power outage in Bear Lake County on the west side of the valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage and warning level winds occurred with the passage of a cold front the evening of March 10th. A roof was blown off a building in American Falls into power lines with power outages in the city for up to an hour.  Wind speeds of 78 mph were measured in American Falls.  The winds also downed some power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure off the Pacific Northwest Coast steered a deep fetch of subtropical moisture into the Idaho Panhandle which persisted from the end of March through April 7th bringing several rounds of heavy rain.  Mild temperatures and breezy winds accompanying the subtropical moisture feed resulted in excessive mid-elevation snowmelt. The combination of heavy rainfall and excessive runoff resulted in sharp rises in most basins within the Idaho Panhandle.  In addition, saturated soils became unstable in some locations resulting in debris flows.  There were no injuries or reports of damage to residences or businesses however, significant damage was observed along transportation infrastructure within Bonner, Boundary, Lewis, Nez Perce, and Shoshone Counties as well as the Nez Perce Reservation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large debris flow which began around noon on March 30th along Forest Road 208 continued to slide as of April 20th. The slide blocked access to six permanent residences.  There was an estimated 25,000 cubic yards of rock and mud over the road.  The estimated cost to remove the earth and fix any damage to the road was 330 thousand dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure off the Pacific Northwest Coast steered a deep fetch of subtropical moisture into the Idaho Panhandle which persisted from March 29th through early April. This resulted in several rounds of moderate to heavy precipitation, which primarily fell as rain below 6000 feet.  Mild temperatures and breezy winds accompanying the subtropical moisture feed resulted in excessive mid-elevation snowmelt.  The combination of heavy rainfall and excessive snow melt resulted in sharp rises in most basins within the Central Idaho Panhandle and Idaho Palouse.   In addition, saturated soils became unstable in some locations resulting in debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large debris flows occurred around noon along Forest Road 208. The first slide was near the intersection of Lost Creek.  The second slide was near the intersection of Clee Circle Road.  No injuries were reported however 14 residents were trapped due to impassible roads and two of these residents had to be evacuated by the sheriff for medical appointments.  The road blocked access to six permanent residences.  There was an estimated 25,000 cubic yards of rock and mud over the road with the larger slide and 8000 cubic yards associated with the smaller slide.  The debris slide was still sliding the first week of April.  The estimated cost to remove the earth and fix any damage to the road was 330 thousand dollars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A culvert measuring 24 inches in diameter and 50 feet in length became plugged allowing water to run over Old Coeur D Alene River Road.  The water washed out some of the shoulder and road surface.  The culvert was severely damaged and will need to be replaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system off the Pacific Northwest coast resulted in a strong northeast surface pressure gradient across northern Idaho on the 14th of May. Subsequently, northeast winds in the neighborhood of 15-35 mph sustained with gusts between 30 and 40 mph were observed at reporting stations throughout the area.  These winds were even stronger along the eastern shores of Priest Lake including the Indian Creek Campground, Cape Horn Road, Pinto Point, Huckleberry Bay, and portions of Coolin, ID where roughly 300 trees were downed or snapped.  Winds were estimated to be at least 60 mph in these localized areas.  Terrain channeling was thought to be responsible for the localized high winds.  Each area that sustained damage was located at the end of deep east to west oriented valleys.  In addition, saturated soils from an unseasonable wet spring would have also contributed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately three-hundred trees were toppled at the Indian Creek Campground located within Priest Lake State Park. Trees began to fall around 1530PST and were still falling at midnight. Some trees were snapped halfway down and others were toppled completely. The park ranger stated you could hear the trees snapping along the ridge-tops and the wind and damage would work its way down the valleys in waves with some damage swaths as long as a football field. This was the worst tree damage he had seen since he started working at the park in 1984. Campers had to be evacuated around 1700PST. Within the campground, twelve picnic tables were destroyed; one electrical pedestal, and a camper's truck and camper sustained some damage. The campground was forced to close for two weeks for cleanup operations.  Downed trees were also reported along Cape Horn Road, Pinto Point, Huckleberry Bay, and in the town of Coolin, ID.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet spring season resulted in saturated soils across the Inland Northwest.  Rivers, small streams, and creeks began to rise as temperatures warmed on May 13th and 14th as snowmelt infiltrated many watersheds across the Central Panhandle. Warm and dry weather transitioned to cooler but considerably wetter conditions over the weekend on May 15th into early the next week.  An upper level low pumped a considerable amount of moisture into the region with an extended period of heavy rain falling Sunday into Monday.  Forty-eight hour rainfall totals were up to between three-quarters and one inch for many locations across the Coeur d'Alene Area and the Central Panhandle Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man became stranded as the North Fork of the Coeur d'Alene River flooded Sunday morning.  The camper and his trailer had to be pulled out 1,000 feet through 3 1/2 feet of water by a tow truck.  Property damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eastbound lanes of Highway 4 were flooded between Gem Hill and Frisco Roads at mile post 5.  Roadway was reduced to one lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of warm temperatures and series of wet storm systems brought rises to many rivers, creeks, and streams across portions of the Northern Idaho Panhandle.  Sharp rises were observed along the Coeur D Alene River near Cataldo on the evening of May 22nd before the river receded on the 24th.  Several roads along the river were inundated with water and a collapse culvert along Highway 3 during the past winter allowed more water than normal to flow into Rose Lake and surrounding community.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the Coeur D Alene River resulted in several roads being inundated with water between Rose Lake and Cataldo.  Approximately three-quarters of a mile of Rose Creek Road from Highway 3 had nearly a foot and a half of water over it and was closed for several days.  A mile of Dudley Road, just west of Cataldo, was under a foot of water.  The community of Rose Lake itself at Highway 3 experienced some backflow from the Coeur D Alene River, causing a slight build-up of water up to some houses foundations.  The west end of King Street in Rose Lake was completely covered in water.  One resident living at the end of King Street reported having as much as three to four feet of water against his foundation.  The only damage to his property was to sheetrock in his garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cool storm system and ample moisture resulted in moderate to heavy precipitation across the Northern Idaho Panhandle on the 26th of May.  Following a cold front passage the morning of the 26th, snow levels lowered near 4000 feet resulting in late-season heavy snow over the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts from SNOTEL sites across the Central Panhandle Mountains reported anywhere from 0.50 to 1.20 inches of liquid.  Those sites with snow pillows recorded 4 to 10 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cool storm system and ample moisture resulted in moderate to heavy precipitation across the Northern Idaho Panhandle on the 26th of May.  Following a cold front passage the morning of the 26th, snow levels lowered near 4000 feet resulting in late-season heavy snow over the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts from SNOTEL sites across the Central Panhandle Mountains reported anywhere from 0.80 to 1.80 inches of liquid.  Those sites with snow pillows recorded 4 to 10 inches of snowfall.  Schweitzer Ski Resort received eight inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough sagged down the British Columbia coastline and eventually into the Pacific Northwest.  Ahead of the trough, moderate to strong west to southwest flow in the lower levels focused strong orographic flow into the Idaho Panhandle resulting in twenty four to sixty hours of heavy snow.  During the two day period, the highest snowfall accumulations were generally above two thousand feet where reports ranged between one and two feet.  Lower elevations generally experienced four to eight inches.  Silver Mountain Ski Resort was forced to close on the 27th due to high winds and white out conditions.  Heavy snow continued into March 1st for the Central Panhandle Mountains, Idaho Palouse, and Camas Prairie.

A long duration heavy snow event in the Idaho Panhandle began on the 21st of February and came to an end on the 24th.  A very cold upper-level storm system settled into the Pacific Northwest during the 21st and 22nd of February.  Numerous disturbances that moved across the region produced several rounds of heavy snow across the Idaho Panhandle.  Most locations experienced 36 to 56 hours of snowfall over the four day period with valley locations receiving between one and two feet of snow while the mountains received upwards of two to five feet.  A strong arctic front moved through the region on the evening of the 23rd.  A combination of heavy snow bands developing along the front followed by strong northeast winds behind the front resulted in blizzard conditions for several hours across Boundary, Bonner, and Kootenai County's before the storm subsided the morning of the 24th.  Law enforcement reported that whiteout conditions contributed to 85 car accidents and 50 vehicle slide-offs.  Several highways were temporarily closed due to drifting snow including highway 195, highway 41 between Post Falls and Rathdrum, and westbound lanes of Interstate 90 near the highway 41 exit.  Most school districts cancelled school on February 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 47 mph were recorded at the Sandpoint Airport with frequent visibilities of a quarter mile.  A trained spotter in Naples, Idaho reported six birch trees, approximately six inches in diameter, were blown down due to the strong winds.  Additional reports from trained spotters and law enforcement confirmed blowing snow and large drifts along highway 95 from Bonners Ferry to Athol, Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level system brough localized heavy snow in the Pocatello metro area with 5 to 7 inches reported throughout the Pocatello area.  The timing of the snow which the majority fell between 4 am and 7 am on the 25th caused a two hour delay in the Pocatello/Chubbuck local school district 25 and several private schools in Pocatello to be closed.   National Weather Service employees reported 6.5 inches in West Chubbuck and 6.0 inches in East Pocatello.   3 inches fell in American Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level system brough localized heavy snow in the Pocatello metro area with 5 to 7 inches reported throughout the Pocatello area.  The timing of the snow which the majority fell between 4 am and 7 am on the 25th caused a two hour delay in the Pocatello/Chubbuck local school district 25 and several private schools in Pocatello to be closed.   National Weather Service employees reported 6.5 inches in West Chubbuck and 6.0 inches in East Pocatello.   3 inches fell in American Falls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of traffic accidents were reported in the Silver Valley due to this storm, which carried over into March 1st.  The Idaho State Police reported seven crashes and fourteen slide-offs.  One fatality was reported near Mullan, ID, but the gender and age of the deceased person is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A parade of storm systems moved along a deep surge of subtropical moisture aimed into the Northern Idaho Panhandle March 10th through the 11th.  This resulted in periods of moderate to heavy rainfall and mild, breezy conditions.  The combination of heavy rain and melting snow resulted in sharp rises of Paradise Creek in the Idaho Palouse with minor flooding observed in the city of Moscow.  Upstream sensors of the Paradise Creek Basin reported over an inch of rainfall over the two day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was observed along Bridge Street in Moscow as water overflowed its banks along Paradise Creek.   The City of Moscow utilized sandbags and temporary gravel walls to contain the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air was trapped in the valleys of north central Idaho as a strong Pacific storm system moved over the region. This system had abundent moisture with waves of energy moving across Idaho. The weather associated with this system covered a wide range of types, from freezing rain to heavy snow and a mixture of rain and snow, along with another blast of freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chains were required on Highway 12 over Lolo Pass.

No injuries were reported however, the truck's fuel tank was punctured spilling one hundred gallons of diesel fuel along the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into Southwest Idaho on the 25th spreading heavy snow across parts of Twin Falls County, the Western Magic Valley and the Southwest Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 6 inches of new snow around Hollister and Rogerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into Southwest Idaho on the 25th spreading heavy snow across parts of Twin Falls County, the Western Magic Valley and the Southwest Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 12 inches of new snow around Riddle and Grasmere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into Southwest Idaho on the 25th spreading heavy snow across parts of Twin Falls County, the Western Magic Valley and the Southwest Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 4 to 8 inches of new snow including 4 inches near Buhl, 5 in Jerome and 8 inches on the southwest side of Twin Falls.

A combination of heavy snow from the previous two days, single digit temperatures causing the snow to become light and fluffy, and strong northeast winds resulted in widespread areas of snow and blowing snow.

Most school districts cancelled school on February 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts varied from four to eight inches with isolated locations receiving around a foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration heavy snow event in the Idaho Panhandle began on the 21st of February and came to an end on the 24th.  A very cold upper-level storm system settled into the Pacific Northwest during the 21st and 22nd of February.  Numerous disturbances that moved across the region produced several rounds of heavy snow across the Idaho Panhandle.  Most locations experienced 36 to 56 hours of snowfall over the four day period with valley locations receiving between one and two feet of snow while the mountains received upwards of two to five feet.  A strong arctic front moved through the region on the evening of the 23rd.  A combination of heavy snow bands developing along the front followed by strong northeast winds behind the front resulted in blizzard conditions for several hours across Boundary, Bonner, and Kootenai County's before the storm subsided the morning of the 24th.  Law enforcement reported that whiteout conditions contributed to 85 car accidents and 50 vehicle slide-offs.  Several highways were temporarily closed due to drifting snow including highway 195, highway 41 between Post Falls and Rathdrum, and westbound lanes of Interstate 90 near the highway 41 exit.  Most school districts cancelled school on February 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coeur D'Alene airport reported sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 40 mph along with visibilities at or below half a mile due to snow and blowing snow.  Travel was severely disrupted along highway 95, highway 41, and Interstate 90 due to blowing snow.  All school districts were closed on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active northwest flow set up over Idaho bringing a series of shortwaves into the region over a couple of days. This produced areas of heavy snow, especially in areas that were orographically enhanced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dixie co-op observer measured 5.5 inches of snow from the evening of the 13th until the evening of the 14th. Another 6.8 inches of snow was measured from the evening of the 14th to the evening of the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active northwest flow set up over Idaho bringing a series of shortwaves into the region over a couple of days. This produced areas of heavy snow, especially in areas that were orographically enhanced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lolo Pass SNOTEL reported 16 inches of snow 7 miles SW of Lolo Hot Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways estimated 7 to 9 inches of snow over Lost Trail Pass on Highway 93 and Chief Joseph Pass on Highway 43.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather disturbances moved across Idaho over a couple of days. Abundent moisture was associated with these disturbances along with a very strong jet steam. Many locations received heavy snow, especially in orographically enhanced areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-thunderstorm wind gust of 67 mph was reported by the High Camp weather station, 4 miles S of Grangeville. The sustained wind was from the southwest at 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather disturbances moved across Idaho over a couple of days. Abundent moisture was associated with these disturbances along with a very strong jet steam. Many locations received heavy snow, especially in orographically enhanced areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 5.1 inches of snow 19 miles S of Elk City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather disturbances moved across Idaho over a couple of days. Abundent moisture was associated with these disturbances along with a very strong jet steam. Many locations received heavy snow, especially in orographically enhanced areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 3 inches of snow 3 miles NE of Orofino.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and wet period resulted in small stream and main stem river flooding.  The Inland Northwest remained in the warm sector of a flat ridge with warm air advection into the region.  The warm air scoured out the cold air in place, resulting in sharp rises to snow levels across the Idaho Panhandle.  Record November snowfall followed by above average December snowfall set the stage for a healthy low to mid elevation snowpack for much of the central and northern Idaho Panhandle.  In these areas, snow levels rose sharply during this period as a series of wet weather systems produced heavy rainfall at times at mid and low elevations.  Heavy rain, combined with the melting snow pack, resulted in small streams, as well as, main stem rivers running above flood stage in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on the Coeur D'Alene River along Coeur D'Alene River Road into Enaville and downstream to the border of Kootenai County near Cataldo.  Coeur D'Alene River Road was closed between mile post 2 and 19 due to flooding of three to four feet of water over portions of the roadway.  Other reports of flooding occurred at the north end of Montgomery Gulch and Moon Gulch.  Moon Gulch road was washed out crossing Beaver Creek trapping an individual on his property, multiple motor vehicles and homes flooded, and landslides were also reported.  The Coeur D'Alene River at Cataldo went above its flood stage of 43 feet at 2330PST on the 16th; crested at 45.94 feet at 2345PST on the 17th; and then went back below flood stage at 415PST on the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight surface pressure gradient with an associated strong cold front produce very strong winds across Lemhi county Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salmon RAWS on a hill above the city of Salmon recorded a gust to 54 mph, at an elevation of 5100 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a strong cold front moved across Idaho on the 12th, abundent moisture moved into the region from the southwest, producing heavy snowfall at the pass levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Grangeville measured 12 inches of snowfall in 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to nine inches of snow was reported with the first round of snow which fell between the 21st and 22nd.  A second wave of moisture resulted in two to five inches of snow between the 22nd and 23rd.  A third wave of moisture interacted with an arctic boundary the evening of the 23rd into the morning of the 24th resulting in an additional three to eight inches of snow.  Four day snowfall totals in the valleys ranged from eight to fifteen inches.  In the mountains, three to five feet of snow fell over the four day period with the most impressive total coming from Lookout Ski Resort which received 52 inches of new snow throughout the period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along the Coeur D'Alene River in Cataldo resulting in road closers of CCC Road and Latour Road.  The flooding in Cataldo was the western most extent of moderate flooding along the Coeur D'Alene River.  More extensive damage was felt upstream in Shoshone County.  Minor flooding continued further downstream from Cataldo to Harrison along the Coeur D'Alene River.  High water levels and strong currents on the Coeur D'Alene River caused a float home and two boat houses to break free from the Harrison Dock Builders (HDB) Marine in Harrison and drift down river into Coeur D'Alene Lake.  The Coeur D'Alene River at Cataldo went above its flood stage of 43 feet at 2330PST on the 16th; crested at 45.94 feet at 2345PST on the 17th; and then went back below flood stage at 415PST on the 19th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along the St. Joe River from Avery to Calder.  Avery Creek rose above its banks and flooded parts of the old Forest Service building and nearby residences in the town of Avery.  The old highway from Marble Creek to Avery was washed out as well.  Mudslides and high water wiped out sections of Calder Road and Trout Creek Road near Calder.  More flooding was reported along the St. Joe River downstream of Calder into Benewah County.  On the 17th, the St. Joe River at Calder went above its flood stage of 13 feet at 1045PST; crested at 13.18 feet at 1445PST; and then went back below flood stage at 2000PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged southeastward across the region and stalled over the central Idaho Panhandle.  Temperatures quickly dropped to around freezing down to valley floors with rain changing over to snow Tuesday morning.  The mountains continued to see snowfall through the afternoon hours as valleys transitioned to more of a rain/snow mix.  Overnight into Wednesday morning, however, another round of snow was felt for many valley locations as this cold front finally began to push out of the area.  Heavy snowfall was observed for the 24 hour period from Tuesday morning to Wednesday morning in the range of four to eight inches in the valleys and around a foot and a half in the mountains.  Lolo Pass received a storm total snowfall of seventeen inches and Silver Mountain saw fifteen to twenty inches.

The combination of a moist Pacific storm system and the intrusion of cold Arctic air produced a wide range of weather conditions.  Strong and gusty winds produced very cold wind chill temperatures.  Heave snow and wind produced areas of blowing snow, along with isolated near blizzard condition.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were produced by heavy snowfall and winds of 42 mph, 2 miles southwest of Clearwater.

Flooding reported on roadways with road closures in Potlatch along the Palouse River and in Paradise Creek, and on Highway 95 near Flannigan Creek; in Moscow along Paradise Creek; and on Highway 8 at milepost 21 between Troy and Deary.  The Palouse River at Potlach went above its flood stage of 15 feet at 1630PST on the 16th; crested at 16.5 feet at 530PST on the 17th; and then went back below flood stage at 615PST on the 18th.  No damage was reported.

The road to St. Joe City was completely under water and flooding reported five miles up St. Maries River Road at Kittle Loop.  The St. Joe River at St. Maries went above its flood stage of 32.5 feet at 1023PST on the 17th; crested at 35.97 feet at 905PST on the 18th; and then went back below flood stage at 500PST on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of the 28th into the early morning hours of the 29th,  a low-level jet developed over the Idaho Panhandle ahead of a strong low pressure system tracking along the Washington / Canadian Border.  Through midnight, the strong low-level winds remained decoupled from the valley floors with high wind gusts measured only along exposed ridgetops.  Around midnight, a line of thunderstorms developed along the trailing cold front.  A combination of descending air within the thunderstorm downdrafts along with terrain channeling was thought to be responsible for mixing the stronger winds aloft down to some valley locations within the Central Panhandle Mountains of Idaho.  There were two reports of large trees toppled by these winds.  Wind gusts within the zone ranged from 30 to 60 miles per hour with gusts near 60 miles per hour thought to be present where the trees were downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St Maries COOP reported a tree had fallen onto power-lines in the Benewah Valley due to strong wind gusts associated with thunderstorms between 1230 AM PST and 100AM PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A flattening ridge over the eastern Pacific Ocean allowed for a decent fetch of moisture to move into the region out of the British Columbia.  Snowfall was primarily confined to the Northeast Mountains and Idaho Panhandle.  Heavy snowfall was reported in the Northern Panhandle with most other locations across the forecast area receiving lighter amounts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported at Ahsahka Idaho, where 10 inches were measured.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL at Shanghi Summit, elevation of 4600 feet, reported 10 inches of snow.

A cold air mass was firmly in place as a moist warm front moved across the region.  Much of the region saw a mix baggage of precipitation types with the Upper Columbia Basin and the Spokane/Coeur D'Alene Area seeing light freezing rain.  The Northeast Mountains and Northern Panhandle Mountain valleys started out with light to moderate snowfall.  The warmer air intruding in at low to mid levels began to change this snowfall over to a few hours of light freezing rain and/or sleet for the Northeast Mountain Valleys with mainly two to three inch snowfall accumulations reported.  The thermal profile over the Northern Panhandle was cold enough for precipitation to remain as snow through the afternoon with heavy snow reported over the area.

A modified arctic air mass situated over the region was overrun by a subtropical moisture fetch resulting in heavy snow over the Northern Idaho Panhandle.   All locations received heavy snow on the 12th, then over time, the colder air in the valleys mixed out with heavy snow mainly focused above three-thousand feet on the 13th.  Snowfall amounts ranged from four to eight inches in the valleys to over a foot in the mountains.  Most school districts closed school on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of five to six inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist Pacific system interacted with cold Arctic air to produce heavy snowfall across north central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL at Savage Pass, elevation 6171 feet, received 9 inches of snow.

Around midnight, a line of thunderstorms developed along the trailing cold front. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts knocked down at least four large trees in the town of Osburn, Idaho.  The toppled trees caused extensive damage to a motor home, car, storage shed, car port, and patio cover.  One tree clipped a house resulting in minor damage.  Two roofs were completely blown off from primary residences and several other roofs were damaged.  Several power poles and power-lines were damaged.  The Nuckols RAWS, located at 4000 feet just outside the town of Osburn, measured sustained winds of 31 mph with gusts to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist frontal system diving out of British Columbia brought heavy snow to the mountains of North Idaho. Snow levels began near  valley floors then climbed near four thousand feet as warm, moist air flooded into the region. Lookout and Silver Mountain Ski Resorts received eight to twelve inches of new snow.

A strong cold front moving west to east across the region produced strong winds in northern Idaho during the evening hours.  Several reports of wind gusts between 35 to 50 mph were observed in the valleys with one report of a wind gust up to 75 mph at roughly 6,500 feet elevation in the Selkirk Mountains.  Several trees and power lines were reported down during the event.

A strong cold front moving west to east across the region produced strong winds in northern Idaho during the evening hours.  Several reports of wind gusts between 35 to 50 mph were observed in the valleys with one report of a wind gust up to 75 mph at roughly 6,500 feet elevation in the Selkirk Mountains.  Several trees and power lines were reported down during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in the range of 45 to 55 mph were reported from 2000PST through just after midnight.  By 2000PST, 369 customers were reportedly out of power.  A tall ponderosa pine estimated to be three feet in diameter snapped during the event on Trevino Drive in Coeur d'Alene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowpack melting combing with rains allowed many rivers to go above flood stage and flooding to occur in much of southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Snake River at Blackfoot reached flood stage of 10 feet on May 10th and remained over flood stage the remainder of the month peaking at 12.04 feet on May 29th.  The Riverton area had widespread flooding resulting in the closure of Riverton Road with several homes threatened.   The Rose Levee broke on May 27th resulting in the washout of about 200 feet of levee structure.  Flows occurred through the Rose Ponds recreation area flooding campgrounds and a walking path.   The Archery Range Road was flooded causing the evacuation of a resident from their property.  In Blackfoot, sub-water seepage into Jensen's Grove caused minor flooding of the park and an industrial storage lot behind the Super 8 Motel and Blackfoot Medical Center.  Flooding of lowlands and agricultural fields occurred adjacent to the river between Rost and Tilden Bridge.   The Snake River at Shelley reached flood stage on May 16th at 12 feet and peaked at 13.15 feet on May 28th which was major flood stage.  County Road 700 North southwest of Firth was closed.  In firth, the Riverview Arena was partially underwater and extensive flooding of lowlands and agricultural fields occurred adjacent to the river between Firth and Rose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two reports of over 60 mph winds in Jefferson reported with severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty-four mph wind gust at the Rover site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow pounded parts of the central Panhandle as a moist frontal boundary stalled over the region.  This is the same frontal boundary that sagged southward the previous day on February 28th with heavy snowfall totals over the Central Panhandle Mountains, Idaho Palouse, and Camas Prairie before heading into the first day of March.  This band slowly migrated northward through the morning hours with heavy snowfall ending over the Central Panhandle from south to north.  Many valley locations received a foot or more or total snowfall from February 28th into March 1st.

A stationary trough of low pressure off the Pacific Northwest Coast steered a deep fetch of subtropical moisture into the Idaho Panhandle which persisted from the end of March through April 7th bringing several rounds of heavy rain.  Mild temperatures and breezy winds accompanying the subtropical moisture feed resulted in excessive mid-elevation snowmelt. The combination of heavy rainfall and excessive runoff resulted in sharp rises in most basins within the Idaho Panhandle.  In addition, saturated soils became unstable in some locations resulting in debris flows.  There were no injuries or reports of damage to residences or businesses however, significant damage was observed along transportation infrastructure within Bonner, Boundary, Lewis, Nez Perce, and Shoshone Counties as well as the Nez Perce Reservation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of Lawyer's Creek resulted in closures of Lawyer's Creek Road between highway 162 and Flying B Ranch.  The main creek split with one flow going toward Flying B Ranch where one resident reported at least one foot of water around his house.  The rest of the creek flowed eastward to Robinson's Pond where it ate away at the levy between the pond and highway spilling about three feet of water over the road.

Several rounds of moisture pushed through as multiple systems impacted northern Idaho as each moved in off the eastern Pacific. Although some light valley snowfall occurred during the morning hours on March 9th, these were relatively warm systems with the heaviest snow falling across the mountains. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the morning and afternoon hours on March 10th as the strongest and wettest system moved through the region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall across the mountains was estimated at around a foot and a half to over two feet from the morning hours on March 9th through the early morning hours on March 11th.  Silver Mountain ski resort picked up 28 inches of total snowfall from these systems and Lookout Pass ski resort picked up 15 inches.

The combination of heavy rainfall and excessive runoff resulted in sharp rises in most basins within the Idaho Panhandle.  In addition, saturated soils became unstable in some locations resulting in debris flows.  There were no injuries or reports of damage to residences or businesses however, significant damage was observed along transportation infrastructure within Bonner, Boundary, Lewis, Nez Perce, and Shoshone Counties as well as the Nez Perce Reservation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 inch culvert separated under a two lane paved road (Dobson Pass, FR 456) on a steep hill causing the slope to slide down the hill taking one half of the paved lane with it.  The fill slope failure was 160 feet long and the paved roadway failure is 80 feet long.  At the Dobson Pass Narrows, the outside fill slope was sliding down the hill reducing the road to one lane.  The road shoulder was gone and pavement was undermined.  The site was 300 feet long.  The slide may have begun with heavy rainfall from March 31st and April 1st but was not discovered until April 2nd.  The estimated cost of damage to the roadway was $75,000.

There were no injuries or reports of damage to residences or businesses however, significant damage was observed along transportation infrastructure within Bonner, Boundary, Lewis, Nez Perce, and Shoshone Counties as well as the Nez Perce Reservation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house in the Waha area (40486 Hawkview Lane) and its hillside had minor sliding.  Residents at the location were evacuated by the Sheriff's Office.  Exact time of the slide was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure off the Pacific Northwest Coast steered a deep fetch of subtropical moisture into the Idaho Panhandle which persisted from the end of March through April 7th bringing several rounds of heavy rain.  Mild temperatures and breezy winds accompanying the subtropical moisture feed resulted in excessive mid-elevation snowmelt. The combination of heavy rainfall and excessive runoff resulted in sharp rises in most basins within the Idaho Panhandle.  In addition, saturated soils became unstable in some locations resulting in debris flows.  There were no injuries or reports of damage to residences or businesses however, significant damage was observed along transportation infrastructure within Bonner, Boundary, Lewis, Nez Perce, and Shoshone Counties as well as the Nez Perce Reservation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coeur D Alene Press reported that the St Maries River flooded it banks at Fernwood covering a manhole in town, leaking wastewater into the river.   There were no direct impacts to health or wildlife.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low in the Gulf of Alaska pumped in copious amounts of moisture with sub-tropical origins.  Orographics played a major role in creating the lift that produced the precipitation over the Central Panhandle Mountains.  Snow increased to between 3500 and 4000 feet during the day, which kept much of the heaviest snowfall in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOTELS in the area received approximately a half to one foot of total snowfall between Monday morning and early Tuesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet spring resulted in saturated soils across northern Idaho.  Recent heavy rainfall on the 25th and 26th of April was too much for a hillside along East Hayden Lake Road.  Rainfall totals for the event ranged from three-quarters of an inch to an inch and a half across this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide along East Hayden Lake Road at mile post 5.5 resulted in half of the road breaking off and sliding down the embankment.  Time of the slide is approximated.  Lakes Highway District reported that the slide occurred sometime between their morning and afternoon inspections.  Exact cost of road repairs is unknown, but it is thought that the total cost will be between 50 and 100 thousand dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second heavy snowfall event occurred in as many days as another round of moisture moved into the region.  Strong lift and abundant moisture resulted in snow beginning to fall during the nighttime hours of April 5th.  By the morning hours of April 6th, a cold front swung in and stalled temporarily over the area, resulting in continued heavy snowfall over the Central Panhandle Mountains.  Although snow levels dropped to between 1,500 and 2,000 feet with cold frontal passage, much of the heaviest snowfall occurred ahead of the front with heavy snowfall primarily remaining at higher elevations above 3,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOTELS in the area recorded about a half an inch of total snowfall during the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air was trapped in the valleys of north central Idaho as a strong Pacific storm system moved over the region. This system had abundent moisture with waves of energy moving across Idaho. The weather associated with this system covered a wide range of types, from freezing rain to heavy snow and a mixture of rain and snow, along with another blast of freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer in Powell Idaho measured 8.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and complex weather system from the Pacific moved across north central Idaho to end the year. This system had three major impacts on the region. First, a tight surface pressure gradient, along with strong upper level winds produced high surface winds. Second, abundant moisture with this system produced some heavy snowfall over the mountains, but since the air mass was warm, the lower elevations received a mix of rain and snow. Third, the warm, moist air mass produced areas of heavy rain which produced rock slides and the release of ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 mph was reported at the Grangeville airport with sustained winds out of the southwest at 47 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Grangeville airport. Also Grangeville 1 n reported a south wind of 39 mph with a gust to 58 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Skull Gulch RAWS, 11 miles west-southwest of Shoup, reported a gust of 81 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the northern Idaho Panhandle from the southwest through the late afternoon and evening hours on November 16th. This front acted to moisten the atmosphere ahead of a cold front that moved into the area during the overnight hours on the sixteenth. Heaviest snowfall occurred out ahead and along the cold front with up to five to seven inches of new snowfall within a five hour span that stretched into the early morning hours of November 17th.
The Salmon River Road was closed by snow slides. Heavy snow loads and rain caused numerous slides between Pine Creek Bridge and the mouth of Panther Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road crews reported that one of the avalanches blocked nearly a quarter mile section of the road near Halfway Gulch with snow 10 feet deep on the downhill side of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a wildfire near the Selkirk Crest, about 20 miles northwest of Bonners Ferry.  Dry and breezy conditions fanned the flames as the fire grew to 600 acres on September 16th.  The wildfire burned in heavy fuels consisting of mixed conifer forest.  The steep mountainous terrain made the fire difficult to fight and remained active through the end of the month with a total of 727 acres consumed.  Two private structures were threatened, but no damage was reported.
A lightning strike started a wildfire just after noon on September 29th.  The fire continued to burn through the middle of October and consumed a total of 1,385 acres of forest timber.

A combination of surface heating and weak ascent ahead of a compact upper-level low drifting into north-central Oregon resulted in scattered thunderstorms over Northeastern Oregon on the afternoon of the 28th.  Meanwhile, very dry boundary layer conditions were developing over North Idaho.  By the late afternoon and early evening, southerly steering flow directed the storms into the Idaho Panhandle.  While gusty outflow winds were common with most storms, two storms were thought to have produced a microburst resulting in localized damage in each of Nez Perce and Kootenai Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A highly localized microburst or possible gustnado resulted in minor damage of the Eagle Point subdivision approximately four miles east of downtown Lewiston. A section of a sheet metal roof was lifted off a two-story shop, a boat trailer was pushed into the outbuilding denting the sheet metal, three two-inch diameter trees were snapped, a travel trailer was knocked down, and lawn objects became airborne breaking one window of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning set off many wildfires across parts of Southwest Idaho on the evening of the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Boise BLM dispatch reported 9 new wildfires in Ada and Elmore Counties as a result of lightning on the evening of the 14th. The wildfires continued to burn overnight until the morning of the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning set off many wildfires across parts of Southwest Idaho on the evening of the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning caused wildfire shut down Interstate 84 in both directions near Twin Falls for 2 hours from 845 PM MST to 1045 PM MST. The fire continued to burn overnight until mid day the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 1 to 2 inch hail were received in Bear Lake County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1.75 inch hail in Wardboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive low pressure system made landfall on the Oregon/California coast early in the morning. There was rapid development of precipitation across Idaho overnight, producing some heavy rainfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 0.78 inches was reporred at Fenn USFS ranger station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and warm Pacific weather system moved into Idaho bringing significant moisture to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The equipment at Savage Pass reported 1.40 inches of rainfall in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive low pressure system made landfall on the Oregon/California coast early in the morning. There was rapid development of precipitation across Idaho overnight, producing some heavy rainfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Lowell reported 0.85 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of orographics and isentropic ascent centered in the warm sector of an approaching Pacific storm system resulted in heavy snowfall across the Northern Idaho Panhandle.  Warmer air eventually moved into the region and snow levels climbed above valley floors with heavy snow continuing in the mountains through midday on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valley snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 7 inches.  A snow plow driver located on Mogul Hill, roughly 5000 feet elevation, reported twelve inches of snow through 1130 AM PST on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to the mountains of the Idaho Panhandle.  Snow levels rose near 4000 feet during the event with snowfall amounts ranging anywhere from 8 to 11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOTEL sites from the Northern Idaho Panhandle measured 0.60 to 1.30 inches of liquid precipitation during the event.  Temperatures were in the upper teens to lower twenties |Fahrenheit suggesting precipitation fell as snow.  Using a rough 10 to 1 snow to liquid ratio, most locations received eight to eleven inches.  This was confirmed by the Northern Rockies Avalanche Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive low pressure system made landfall on the Oregon/California coast early in the morning. There was rapid development of precipitation across Idaho overnight, producing some heavy rainfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observation equipment at Shanghi Summit reported 1.00 inch of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive low pressure system made landfall on the Oregon/California coast early in the morning. There was rapid development of precipitation across Idaho overnight, producing some heavy rainfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Equipment at Pierce reported 0.81 inches of rainfall and Pierce USFS ranger station reported 0.70 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low pressure system moved into southwestern Washington.  Bands of snow developed along a TROWAL (TROugh of Warm Air ALoft) that extended from northern Idaho into eastern Washington. This feature was nearly stationary overnight on November 17th through November 18th.  Snow showers persisted into the early hours of November 19th before this feature weakened and shifted eastward.  Heavier snowfall reports were up to around six inches in the valleys to up to a foot in the Central Panhandle Mountains.

Over a two day period, light to moderate snow consistently fell over the Central Panhandle Mountains of Idaho.  During the first 24 hours, snow was of the heavy wet consistency, attributed to a deep subtropical moisture fetch aimed into the region.  A cold front moved through on the 24th and snow processes became more convective and orographically driven. Given the subtropical moisture tap in place, snow mainly fell above 5000 feet until the morning of the 24th when snow levels lowered near 4000 feet.  Over the two-day period, snow amounts measured at area ski resorts ranged from 18 to 21 inches.

A weak disturbance moving through the region kicked off some scattered convection over the central Idaho Panhandle during the early afternoon hours.  One thunderstorm in particular quickly developed east of Genesee and produced a short lived rope like funnel cloud.

The southern and central portions of the Idaho Panhandle was under an unstable and active weather pattern as a cold upper level trough approached from the west and warm, moist air streamed across the area from the southwest.  Solar heating through the late afternoon hours was enough for air parcels to break through the capping inversion with scattered convection firing up through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across eastern Nez Peerce County during the late afternoon hours.  This storm dropped several one inch hail stones mixed in with marble and pea-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure off the Pacific Northwest Coast steered a deep fetch of subtropical moisture into the Idaho Panhandle which persisted from the end of March through April 7th bringing several rounds of heavy rain.  Mild temperatures and breezy winds accompanying the subtropical moisture feed resulted in excessive mid-elevation snowmelt. The combination of heavy rainfall and excessive runoff resulted in sharp rises in most basins within the Idaho Panhandle.  In addition, saturated soils became unstable in some locations resulting in debris flows.  There were no injuries or reports of damage to residences or businesses however, significant damage was observed along transportation infrastructure within Bonner, Boundary, Lewis, Nez Perce, and Shoshone Counties as well as the Nez Perce Reservation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of several creeks and streams resulted in damage, closures, and evacuations along numerous roads along with minor flooding of six homes.  Roads that were damaged include Webb Road, McCormack Ridge Road, Garden Gulch Road near Highway 95, Cottonwood Creek Road, Flatiron Road, and Mission Creek Road near Highway 95.  Reservation Line Road and Webb Canal Road were also closed for a short time due to flooding.  The worst of the flooding occurred along Webb Creek which flooded several low-lying areas, including the junction with Highway 95 and the junction with McCormick Ridge Road where reports stated water covered the bridge at the junction of McCormick Ridge Road.  The Web Store Smoke Shop was inundated with approximately one foot of water.  Around six homes along Webb Road experienced flooding up to the homes foundations.  Mission Creek also spilled out of its banks with water reported to be flowing down Mission Creek Road, over the Mission Creek Bridge, and into nearby pastures and front yards. Sweetwater Creek, Lapwai Creek and Cottonwood Creek also had minor flooding with the Spalding church suffering the greatest damage.  A culvert was plugged along Lapwai Creek near the intersection of Highway 95 and Garden Gulch Road causing road damage and a two day road closure.  A plugged culvert along Sweetwater Creek led to approximately 30 feet of pavement erosion along Flatiron Road and 1500 feet of ditch and shoulder erosion.  The Emergency Manager of Nez Perce County estimated flood damage to the roads was around $74,000 with most of the damage along Flatiron Road and Garden Gulch Road near Highway 95.  On the Nez Perce Indian Reservation, at least one septic tank was compromised due to flooding.  The flooding caused basement flooding in at least four homes throughout the reservation.  Two private bridges were destroyed or compromised and classified as unsafe for use.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed low pressure system off the West Coast produced hail and strong thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kriley Creek RAWS measured a gust of 73 mph.  This site is at 5200 feet elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed low pressure system off the West Coast produced hail and strong thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A police officer estimated 1.50 inches of rain in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large closed low pressure system off the Pacific Northwest coast rotated abundant moisture into Lemhi County Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 1 inch of rain was recorded by the Leadore Creek RAWS within an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a very wet April and May, warmer June temperatures began to melt an above normal mountain snowpack increasing flows into already swollen river basins.  |This caused minor to locally moderate flooding along Lake Pend Oreille and Pend Oreille River along with several of its smaller tributaries. Agriculture fields, recreation sites, roadways, as well as homes were impacted by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor spring flooding occurred along the Pend Oreille River, Clark Fork River, and Lake Pend Oreille.  Johnson Creek Road on the south side of the Clark Fork River had some sloughing.  In central and western areas of the county, Priest River inundated portions of LeClerc Road just east of Oldtown as well as Dufort Road, just east of Priest Lake.  Numerous docks and lakeside yards were flooded by high water levels along the shores of Lake Pend Oreille.  In Sandpoint, ID the basement of the Old Power House was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on August 21st a thunderstorm caused trees to into a house on Pioneer Road in Idaho Falls killing an 82 year old man in his bedroom.  Trees were also downed on East Lincoln Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early on August 21st a thunderstorm caused trees to into a house on Pioneer Road in Idaho Falls killing an 82 year old man in his bedroom. Trees were also downed on East Lincoln Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of monsoonal moisture moved over Idaho during the day. Good heating during the day created deep instability, and the two combined to produce strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured hail 1.00 in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved into the region and interacted with monsoonal moisture to produce thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pieces of vinyl siding, 4 feet to 6 feet long, were blown off a house. Some small trees were kocked down at the same location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire in Bannock County near Chinese Peak was started by juveniles playing with matches.  It burned 1,280 acres and destroyed one home and adjacent barn.  The fire began at 2 pm MDT on August 15th and was 100 percent contained by August 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire in Bannock County near Chinese Peak was started by juveniles playing with matches.  It burned 1,280 acres and destroyed one home and adjacent barn.  The fire began at 2 pm MDT on August 15th and was 100 percent contained by August 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire that ignited near Highway 95 at mile post 284 was quickly fanned by gusty winds as it moved up the hillside west of Reubens.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A relatively small wildfire of around 75 acres completely destroyed a historic railroad trestle.  The estimated cost to rebuild the trestles and railroad track to its original state was between three and four million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a very wet April and May, warmer June temperatures began to melt an above normal mountain snowpack increasing flows into already swollen river basins.  |This caused minor to locally moderate flooding along Lake Pend Oreille and Pend Oreille River along with several of its smaller tributaries. Agriculture fields, recreation sites, roadways, as well as homes were impacted by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake Pend Oreille spilled out of its banks flooding portions of Lakeview Avenue in Bayview, ID.  The lake enveloped recreational sites, docks, and ramps. There was also minor flooding of yards and the parking lot of the Scenic Bay Marina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bruneau River was in flood for the second time during the month as more heavy rain and snow melt affected the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River gauge readings and reports from local officials indicated that the Bruneau River had minor to moderate flooding from Hot Springs to Bruneau from the 14th to the 18th of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure off the Pacific Northwest Coast steered a deep fetch of subtropical moisture into the Idaho Panhandle which persisted from the end of March through April 7th bringing several rounds of heavy rain.  Mild temperatures and breezy winds accompanying the subtropical moisture feed resulted in excessive mid-elevation snowmelt. The combination of heavy rainfall and excessive runoff resulted in sharp rises in most basins within the Idaho Panhandle.  In addition, saturated soils became unstable in some locations resulting in debris flows.  There were no injuries or reports of damage to residences or businesses however, significant damage was observed along transportation infrastructure within Bonner, Boundary, Lewis, Nez Perce, and Shoshone Counties as well as the Nez Perce Reservation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Deep Creek flooded portions of Deep Creek Loop, also listed as Old Highway 95 causing sloughing of the roadway and embankment.  Pavement damage was 500 feet by nine feet wide.  The route is a major county through route from Naples to Bonners Ferry.
